coredata.dc:title: Facile one-step synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots for rapid fingerprint recognition, information encryption, and Fe3+ ion detection

coredata.dc:description: Fingerprint recognition plays a crucial role due to its uniqueness. However, conventional detection methods suffer from significant drawbacks, including high consumption, health risk and sample contamination, which restrict their practical application in the crime investigations and forensic analysis. This necessitates the development of simple, rapid, safe and environmentally-friendly alternative methods for fingerprints detection. In this work, we report the synthesis of novel cyan-emitting carbon dots (CDs) via a facile one-step hydrothermal reaction using polyethyleneimine and coumarin as precursors. The CDs enabled the clear visualization of fingerprint patterns on various substrates through immersion and spraying methods with pure water solutions, without the need for any cosolvents. Level 2 and level 3 details of fingermarks were evidently visible for individual identification within a short exposure time under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Additionally, fluorescent 3D fingerprint images revealed direct and specific evidence regarding pressing habits. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was tunable by adjusting the pH. We further fabricated invisible CDs ink for information encryption and decryption via UV irradiation and acid/alkali treatment. Moreover, CDs have the specific ability to sense Fe3+ ions in actual water samples, with a detection limit of 0.613 μM and a linear range of 0–27 μM. We conducted an in-depth study on the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions, proposing a plausible mechanism based on a synergistic effect of dynamic quenching, static quenching and inner filter effect. Therefore, this study provides a promising multifunctional nanomaterial for fingerprint recognition, data encryption, and Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Facile one-step synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots for rapid fingerprint recognition, information encryption, and Fe3+ ion detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional CDs was developed through a one-step, facile synthesis. Pure aqueous CDs solutions were used to develop latent fingerprints (LFPs) with high-quality details, and fluorescent 3D imaging revealed pressing habits. The CDs also exhibited pH-dependent fluorescence, allowing for dynamic encryption and decryption. Additionally, the CDs demonstrated the ability to detect Fe3+ ions in water samples.

Fingerprint recognition plays a crucial role due to its uniqueness. However, conventional detection methods suffer from significant drawbacks, including high consumption, health risk and sample contamination, which restrict their practical application in the crime investigations and forensic analysis. This necessitates the development of simple, rapid, safe and environmentally-friendly alternative methods for fingerprints detection. In this work, we report the synthesis of novel cyan-emitting carbon dots (CDs) via a facile one-step hydrothermal reaction using polyethyleneimine and coumarin as precursors. The CDs enabled the clear visualization of fingerprint patterns on various substrates through immersion and spraying methods with pure water solutions, without the need for any cosolvents. Level 2 and level 3 details of fingermarks were evidently visible for individual identification within a short exposure time under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Additionally, fluorescent 3D fingerprint images revealed direct and specific evidence regarding pressing habits. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was tunable by adjusting the pH. We further fabricated invisible CDs ink for information encryption and decryption via UV irradiation and acid/alkali treatment. Moreover, CDs have the specific ability to sense Fe3+ ions in actual water samples, with a detection limit of 0.613 μM and a linear range of 0–27 μM. We conducted an in-depth study on the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions, proposing a plausible mechanism based on a synergistic effect of dynamic quenching, static quenching and inner filter effect. Therefore, this study provides a promising multifunctional nanomaterial for fingerprint recognition, data encryption, and Fe3+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Once a hand encounters a substrate’s surface, the ridged patterns of the fingertip leave impressions behind. These ridged patterns, composed of ridge, groove and furrows, are unique personal characteristics to each individual and remain unchanged throughout their lifetime [1,2]. Due to this uniqueness, fingerprints are widely used for personal identification, especially in criminal investigations and forensic analysis, and are considered reliable biometric and scientific evidence [3,4]. Nonetheless, most fingerprints left on surfaces are often less visible or even invisible to the naked eye due to poor optical contrast, these are known as latent fingerprints (LFPs) [5,6]. To make these fingerprints legible, investigators often require additional physical or chemical treatments to enhance visualization. Common methods include powder dusting, ninhydrin spraying, cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, silver nitrate soaking, and vacuum metal deposition [7–13]. Nevertheless, these traditional methods still have drawbacks such as weak contrast, low discrimination, water insolubility, background contaminants, lengthy processing times, and potential health risks [14–16]. For example, powder dusting, one of the most used techniques, requires large amounts of powder, which could inevitably destroy LFPs characteristics during brushing and cause damage to the eyes and respiratory system of the testers [17,18]. The toxicity of heavy metals in detection materials like Cd and Pb cannot be ignored [13,19]. Additionally, chemical staining methods need long periods and heating to develop LFPs images, and the organic reagents used in the chemical treatments can produce harmful volatile organic gases [20]. Consequently, it is urgent to develop novel materials that can rapidly, precisely, and safely visualize LFPs on various surfaces in a cost-effective and large-scale operable manner.

To date, photoluminescent materials such as quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs), organic and inorganic metal complexes, and up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have been frequently used for fingerprint detection owning to their unique fluorescence properties [11,21]. Among these materials, CDs have gained significant attention because of their stable luminescence, tunable excitation and emission spectra, strong photo-bleaching resistance, facile and green synthesis, low cost, good biosafety, and environmental friendliness [22–24]. However, to achieve clear visualization of LFPs, large quantities of CDs powder are required during the staining process, and inhalation of dust particles is unavoidable. Therefore, it necessitates the development of well-designed fluorescent CDs solutions to implement for fingermark by immersing or spraying methods. Currently, a few teams are involved to disperse the fluorescent CDs in various media such as hydrochloric acid [25], nanocomposites aqueous solutions [26], and organic solvents [27,28] to identify LFPs. It is worth noting that selecting an appropriate dissemination medium with a moderate pH is crucial to avoid corrosion of the LFPs surfaces and substrates. Moreover, for environmental-friendly motives, pure water-based inks could minimize the release of harmful compounds into the environment and be nondestructive to fingerprint residues and object surfaces. But until now there are few reports on the use of CDs aqueous solutions alone for fingermark detection.

Ferric irons (Fe3+) are one of the essential metal elements in organisms and nature environments [29–31]. However, insufficient or excessive Fe3+ might cause various diseases and threaten ecological security. Therefore, it is necessary to explore an efficient method to accurately detect Fe3+ in environmental, industrial, drinking water, and living cells. Currently, the commonly used analytical methods mainly include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), electrochemical, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP OES), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), etc [32–35]. However, these techniques are usually expensive, and the the sample preparation process is also very complex. Compared with them, fluorescent analysis has the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, quick response, and simple operation [36,37]. As a new kind of zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, CDs could become an ideal fluorescence probe for Fe3+ detection due to their distinguished optical properties and abundant surface functional groups. For instance, Jeon et al. synthesized Kentucky bluegrass (KB) derived CDs that could emit cyan-blue light by hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in aqueous solution [38]. Han et al. reported that sulfur-nitrogen co-doped dual emission CDs (DECDs), in which Fe3+ ions could quench the fluorescence of DECDs, thus achieving the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions [39].

In the present research, a new type of nitrogen-doped CDs was prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and coumarin as precursors (Scheme 1 ). By varying the mass ratio of PEI to coumarin, a series of CDs were obtained. When the mass ratio was 1:1, the CDs product emitted the strongest green fluorescence under 380 nm irradiation and was named as CP11, with a quantum yield (QY) of 13 %. CP11 shows remarkable capability on LFPs development on different substrates (such as glass, aluminum foil and coin) by immersing in or spraying method using only pure H2O solutions without any organic or inorganic cosolvents. The 1st, 2nd, 3rd level details of LPFs, such as ridge with, furrow characteristics, whorls, islands, bifurcations, loops, arches, and terminations, could be legible distinguished with short incubation time (< 1 min). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CP11 could be dynamically adjusted by modulating the pH of solutions. Based on this feature, our CDs could be changed wilfully in secure communication, making them more reliable for information encryption and anticounterfeiting applications. At the same time, the aqueous solutions of CP11 can be utilized as a highly selective fluorescent probe for Fe3+, with a detection limit of 0.613 μM, showing good performance in determining Fe3+ in real water samples. The facile synthesis of CDs provides multifunctional applications and promising possibilities.

Coumarin, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and ethanol (EtOH), were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemical reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was used throughout all experiments.

The fluorescence spectra and absolute quantum yields were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Dual-FL, Horiba, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured by using FluoroMax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was conducted using IR 200 (Thermo Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on ESCALab 250i spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed on JEM-2100F (JOEL, Japan). The crystal structure was identified by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using XD-6 (Purkinje General Instrument Co., China). Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK) experiments were carried out to measure the size of CDs. Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer was used to identify the 1H NMR spectra. All pH values were gauged applying pH-3c digital pH-meter (Shanghai Lei Ci Device Works, Shanghai, China) with a combined glass-calomel electrode.

Carbon dots (CPx) were prepared using PEI and coumarin as raw materials through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.3 g of PEI and different amounts of coumarin were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol, and the resulting solution was placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel for 9 h’ heating at 90 ℃. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) and then dialyzed (3500 Da) against water for 24 h. Finally, the target CDs solid was obtained by freeze-drying of the dialysis solution. CP11, CP21, CP41, CP15, CP25 and CP45 refer to 1:1, 2:1, 4:1, 1:5, 2:5 and 4:5 ratios of coumarin to PEI, respectively.

Absolute QY was measured by using the calibrated integrating sphere system according to the following equation [28]:

where QY was the absolute quantum yield, f is the photons emitted by sample; α is the photos absorbed by the sample; Eemission is the fluorescence emission spectrum of the sample; Esolvent is the spectrum of light used for excitation with only the solvent, and Esample is the spectrum of the light used to excite the sample. Sample solutions of CDs were used to measure QY in pure water at room temperature, λex = 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of CP11 aqueous solution to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The CP11 solution (1 mg/mL) was adjusted to different pH values using HCl or NaOH solution and the total solution volume was fixed at 3 mL. The final pH values of various solutions were determined to be 1.33, 3.52, 5.47, 7.16, 9.52, 11.32 and 13.35 respectively. Subsequently, their fluorescence emission spectra were measured with an optimum excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and photographs were taken using a mobile phone under 365 nm UV light.

Latent fingerprint samples were visualized as follows: before fingerprints were collected, the donor (female, 22 years old) washed her hands with soap and dried up. Subsequently, she gently rubbed her fingertips over her forehead or nose and stamped them on selected surfaces with minimal pressure to leave fingermarks. The collected samples were aged for 12 h before development. An aqueous solution of CDs (10 mg in 20 mL water) was prepared. Thereafter, LFPs were visualized by CDs solution via immersing or spraying method.
Immersing method: the latent fingermark objects were immersed in CDs solution, and then rinsed with deionized water and allowed to dry naturally.

Spraying method: the CDs solution was transferred into a small spray bottle, then sprayed homogenously from a distance of 5 cm on the surface for a few seconds. The substrates were rinsed with deionized water and allowed to dry naturally.

Ultimately, the developed LPFs images were photographed in situ using a mobile phone under visible and UV 365 nm (10 W) light. All the pictures are of original representations as recorded and no “Photoshop” was applied.

Anti-counterfeit ink was obtained by dissolving CP11 powder in deionized water (0.5 mg/mL), which could be loaded into an empty pen tube and directly used for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. The process of information encryption and decryption is mainly divided into three steps: (a) information import: firstly, the predesigned pattern and words were drawn onto a non-fluorescent commercial paper using our anti-counterfeit ink; (b) information encryption: secondly, the paper was sprayed with hydrochloric acid solution and allowed to dry; (c) information decryption: thirdly, the paper was then sprayed with the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution. The encoded paper was photographed in situ at each step under both normal and UV 365 nm light.

In the ion detection experiment, multiple metal ions and anions stock solutions (1*10−3 M) were prepared in deionized water. Then, each solution (0.1 mL) was separately added to CP11 solutions (1 mg/mL) in a quartz cuvette with a volume ratio of 1:1 and made up to 3 mL to determine the selectivity of CDs. The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions (λ = 380 nm) were measured, and the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm was recorded.

To further verify the selectivity of CP11 for Fe3+, 11 common interfering ions (Pb2+, Na+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, OH−, SO3 2−, CH3COO−, SCN−, I−) were selected to investigated the effects of co-existing ions on the fluorescence of CDs. The volume of binary ion mixtures and measurement procedures were same as single ion.

To evaluate the detection limit of Fe3+ ions, the stock solution of was Fe3+ was diluted into various concentrations (20 ∼ 800 μM). The fluorescence spectrum of the CP11 mixed with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions was also individually tested. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation, and s refers to the slope of the standard curve.

Tap water from the laboratory, lake water from Southwest Minzu University and surface water from the Zoige area of the Tibetan Plateau were collected to analyze the feasibility of CP11 for Fe3+ ions detection in actual environmental samples. The tap water was used directly without any treatment. The lake water and surface water were pretreated with 0.22 μm microporous membranes and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃. The testing process was the same as mentioned above, and all experiments were repeated three times or more.

In this study, PEI and coumarin were chosen as raw materials to prepare CDs by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Coumarin, a significant organic compound with widely application in the chemical and medicinal industries, was combined with hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (PEI), a well water-soluble cationic polymer known for its numerous primary, secondary, and tertiary amine groups. These amine groups are readily prone to enhance the photoluminescence properties of CDs and their affinity for the metal [40–43].

To optimize the reaction condition, the mass of PEI was kept constant at 0.3 g, while the mass of coumarin was varied from 0.06 to 1.2 g. The resulting mass ratios of coumarin to PEI were 1:5, 2:5, 4:5, 1:1, 2:1 and 4:1, and the samples were designated as CP15, CP25, CP45, CP11, CP21 and CP41, respectively. Interestingly, as the mass ratios of coumarin to PEI increased from 1:5 to 1:1, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually. However, further increases in the mass ratio to 2:1 and 4:1 led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). Thus, as shown in Table S1, CDs CP11 with comparatively optimal fluorescence performance and yield were selected for further characterization and applications when equal mass ratio of reactants was used.

The structure, composition and surface functional groups of CP11 were characterized using various techniques, including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The typical HR-TEM image of CP11, as shown in Fig. 1 A, displayed nearly spherical morphologies with a uniform average size of 3.5 nm and excellent dispersibility without aggregation. The inset of HR-TEM image exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, corresponding to the [100] facet of graphitic carbon [44–46]. Additionally, the particle size distribution histogram of dynamic light scattering (DLS) in Fig. 1B disclosed that the particle size distribution was mainly around 2 ∼ 4 nm, consistent with the HR-TEM results. The surface status of CP11 and reactants were characterized via FTIR spectroscopy. As revealed in Fig. 1C, the strong broad characteristic peak in CDs at 3300 cm−1 belonged to the overlap absorption of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. Peaks at 2956 cm−1 and 2837 cm−1 were associated with the C-H stretching vibrations, originating from the alkane skeleton structure of PEI [47,48]. Additional peaks between 1670 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 were ascribed to C = O and C = C stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring, as well as N-H bending vibrations. These results indicate the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups in the CP11 structure, contributing to its excellent water solubility. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2) further confirmed the successful incorporation of PEI and coumarin into the CDs, with proton peaks at 1.5 ∼ 3.5 ppm and 6.5 ∼ 7.5 ppm corresponding to the methylene group of PEI and the aromatic protons of coumarin, respectively. Furthermore, XRD is used to characterize the crystal structure of the CDs. Fig. 1D showed a broad peak centered at about 2θ = 21° indicating the amorphous carbon structure of CP11, consistent with the characteristic peak of graphitic carbon and the HR-TEM results [16,40].

XPS analysis provided further insights into the elemental composition and chemical states of the CDs. As observed from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A), three clear peaks are centered at 284, 398, 531 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, representing the CDs are made up of C, N and O. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) revealed four peaks corresponding to C-C/C = C (284.5 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), C-O (286.0 eV) and C = O (287.6 eV) [49,50]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) was deconvolved into two components at 398.8 eV (N-H) and 399.6 eV (N-C) [51]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), the signals at 531.8 eV and 530.6 eV confirm the presence of O = C and O-C functional groups, respectively [52]. The XPS results were consistent with the FTIR analysis, both of which could corroborate each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and pH responsive behaviors of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the optical properties, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were studied. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A) of the CP11 solution showed two distinct absorption peaks at 278 nm and 380 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of carbon skeleton structure and the n → π* transition of surface functional groups, respectively [53]. As seen from the photo in the inset of Fig. 3A, the CP11 solution exhibits light yellow color under day light (left) and intense yellowish-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation (right). Then, to test the fluorescent characteristics of CP11, the optimal excitation wavelength of the CDs was measured. As presented in the Fig. 3B, the emission intensity was recorded as the excitation wavelength (λex) increased the from 300 nm to 440 nm with an increment of 20 nm. The results showed that CP11 possessed optimum excitation and emission wavelength at 380 nm and 512 nm, respectively, with a large stoke shift of 132 nm. This was consistent with the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectra (Fig. S3). Notably, unlike several previous reports, the fluorescence emission peak position demonstrated negligible shifts with changes in excitation wavelengths, indicating that the CDs were excitation independent. This might be due to the homogenous surface nature and uniform size distribution [54–56].

Furthermore, the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of CP11 was also investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence emission spectra of CP11 in different pH conditions were studied. With the change of pH from 13 to 1, the fluorescence intensity of CP11 at 512 nm is significantly decreased under the excitation of 380 nm, which is consistent with the intensity changes under UV lamp in the inset of Fig. 4. When pH is lower than 5, the fluorescence was almost completely quenched. This pH-dependent fluorescence phenomenon was probably ascribed to the surface functional groups of CP11, such as hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties [3,57,58]. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S4, our CDs demonstrated high photostability even under long-time continuous irradiation.

Fingerprints are essential biometric data used for personal identification, forensic examination and medical diagnosis. Latent fingerprints (LFPs), which are often not readily visible to the naked eye due to the poor optical contrast between the residual secretions on the fingertips and the substrates, present a significant challenge [14,20]. Consequently, the development of the visualization techniques of LFPs has been of great significant importance. Although fluorescent powders have been commonly employed for imaging, they are annoyed by harmful ingredients, inhalation hazard and high reagent consumption. Besides, LFPs found on items that have been wet on the scene of crime are difficult to disclose using fluorescent powders [59]. In this context, solution-based techniques using immersing or spraying methods are preferred.

The process of LFPs development of indeed involves the visualization of informative features of the papillary ridge patterns. The fingerprints physiognomies are generally categorized into three segments, level 1, 2, 3 [19,60]. Level 1 encompasses the general pattern formed by ridges and furrows on the surface, including the sharp and core such as arch, loop, whorl and center point etc. In level 2, more detail feature could be observed, like ridge endings, bi/trifurcation, short ridge, dots, deltas, spurs, lake and so forth, which represent the individuality of the fingerprints. These distinctive points are termed as “minutiae” or “Galton” characteristics. Whereas, the level 3 provides microscopic ridge details of the fingerprints, namely sweat pores, wrinkle, width and narrowness of the ridges, which offers additional reference details for partially defective or blurry level 2 information.

Herein, due to the excellent luminescent property and the uniform small size of the as-prepared CDs, the feasible application of CP11 for rapid LFPs detection was explored. LFPs were collected on a surface, and aged for 12 h were developed using a simple water-solution treatment procedure. Fig. 5 A showed the photoluminescence images of CP11-stained LFPs of a female donor on several typical substrates (glass, aluminum foil, nickel-plated coin) under normal and 365 nm UV light. The influence of developing time, which means the time of substrates immersing in CDs solutions under room temperature, was initially studied. Results indicated that brief immersion times (1 min or 2.5 min) clearly revealed the LFPs details. However, longer immersion times resulted in CDs over-stacking on the LFPs, reducing clarity.

To further investigate the applicability of CP11 solutions in LFPs enhancement, the spraying method was employed. As presented in Fig. 5B, after 365 nm UV light excitation, LFPs on various substrates are legibly presented with the details of patterns, high resolution and good contrast fluorescence signals. Noticeably, LFPs on 1 Chinese Jiao coin exhibited relatively weak contrast and resolution due to their irregular surfaces with both specular reflections and stereoscopic patterns. Unsurprisingly, in the magnified images of Fig. 5C(a)-(i), some minutiae details about the level 2 and level 3 information of LFPs such as the furrow, whorl, island, bifurcation, loop, arch, fork, ridge, termination, sweat pores and so on could be effectively visualized and distinguished. These details are critical in the LFPs identification process, which could help to identify the individual. This spraying process is advantageous as it is not limited by the substrate materials, and offers a large-area operable, safe, quick, hygienic and cost-efficient, approach for LFPs identification in various scenarios. Additionally, 3-dimesional interactive plots of LFPs, as shown in Fig. S5, illustrate that the prepared CDs solution staked exactly on the ridges of LFPs rather than furrows, and did not flow from the center towards the edge without exhibiting a “coffee ring effect”. These acquire 3D image, combined with the minutiae characteristics, could be utilized to infer the pressing habit of fingerprint owner, offering additional evidence at crime scenes [15]. Overall, CP11 pure-water solution is an excellent candidate for rapid visualization of LFPs on various surfaces through simple immersing or spraying methods without the need for cosolvent. Table S2 summarized up to date reported fluorescent materials for latent LFPs detection.

To combat product counterfeiting and intensify information security, fluorescent labeling technology has been widely implemented [61–64]. Unfortunately, similar emission characteristics can be readily simulated or duplicated using other substitutes or some mixtures of fluorescent materials. Thus, inks with more complex security features pose an awkward challenge to the counterfeiters [65]. The unique pH-dependent fluorescence properties of CP11 make it promising candidate for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. The predesigned pattern ‘Panda’ and words ‘SMU’ were drawn onto a non-fluorescent commercial paper using our anti-counterfeit ink. These original pattern and text were invisible under normal light and show clear cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation, as shown in Fig. 6 . Interestingly, the fluorescence massages could be completely masked by treating with an acidic solution, demonstrating good security in information dissemination. For decryption, the encrypted information can be easily and instantly disclosed by treating with an alkaline solution within a short time, and then irradiating with UV light to recover the fluorescence. Therefore, this technology can be used to create anti-counterfeiting patterns on materials such as certificates, labels, package materials, books, notes to achieve advanced anti-counterfeiting applications, and allows for rapid reading and erasing encryption messages through acid-base stimulus responses, thereby to improving the confidentiality in terms of information transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the interaction mechanism of the CDs to pH, the UV–visible avosorption spectra of CP11 solutions under different pH values were measured. As shown in Fig S6A, it was observed that the positions of absorption peaks was affected by the pH value. We found that the absorption peaks at 276 nm are almost constant under weak acid conditions, and a distinct red-shift to 292 nm occurs under the condition of strong acid conditions. Besides, the wide absorption band at 380 nm gradually weakened and disappeared when the pH value was greater than 5. This changes might indicate that surface groups gradually are altered with the increase of pH. Fluorescence lifetime measurements further offered support to our supposition. As expected, the lifetimes were changing with the varying of pH in the Fig S6B. The average lifetimes of CP11, CP11 + HCl and CP11 + HCl + NaOH were measured to be 4.15 ns, 4.49 ns and 3.53 ns, respectively. It was obvious that the lifetime firstly increased and then decreased with the successive addition of acid and base, which further suggested the surface groups of CDs changed and had an effect on the electron transfer process. To explore further, zeta potential measurement is applied. According to Fig. S6C, the zeta potential of CP11 reduces continuously with the increase of the pH and turn to negative when pH rise to 12.8, implying that surface functional groups like amino and carbonyl tend to be protonated or hydrolyzed at high pH value. Besides, when the system is acidic, the dispersibility might be reduced by reducing the charge repulsive force and the fluorescence of the system is also quenched.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: There are plentiful hydrophilic functional groups located on the surface of the CDs that endow CDs with the ability to selectively recognize metal ions [66–69]. To verify the selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ ions, interferences of other ions including K+, Na+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, S2−, SO3 2−, CH3COO−, SCN−, I−, Cl−, F− and forth on were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 A, only Fe3+ ions noticeably affected the fluorescence intensity of CP11, almost utterly quench the fluorescence, while most other ions caused merely minor changes. Additionally, the anti-interference ability of the CDs probes for Fe3+ ions was further explored and the results are shown in Fig. 7B. When other interfering ions coexisted with Fe3+ and probe solutions, negligible influences on the quenched fluorescence of CP11 were observed. The above experimental results indicate that CDs exhibit high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability for detecting Fe3+.

To further assess the sensitivity of CP11 to Fe3+, the Fe3+ stock solution was diluted across a range of concentrations. It was observed in Fig. 8 A that the fluorescence intensity of CP11 gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration. It is to be noted that the emission peak of CP11 did not shift as the fluorescence quenching, indicating that no change occurred in the band structure of CP11. The concentration-dependent quenching coefficient of Fe3+ for CP11 is depicted in Fig. 8B, which provides the plots of (F0-F)/F0 against the concentration from 0 to 27 μM. A good linear curve with the coefficients R2 = 0.9929 was obtained by linear fitting. Besides, the limit of detection (LOD), calculated by the 3 σ/slope method, where σ (0.00591) is the standard deviation, was found to be 0.613 μM. This value is below the maximum content of Fe3+ allowed in surface water and drinking water quality standards, making this CDs with outstanding selectivity and sensitivity highly suitable for efficient Fe3+ detection in actual water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Assessing the ability of fluorescent CDs to detect Fe3+ in environmental sample is of great significance on account of various interference in actual water. Tap water, lake water and surface water samples were chosen for real sample analysis. Different concentrations of Fe3+ were added to these water samples as internal standards and then analyzed. As shown in Table 1 , the measured average recovery rates were all in the range of 97.1 ∼ 111.5 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) values of all less than 8.26 %, indicating the accuracy of the proposed method. Table S3 demonstrated the achievements on synthesis method, working range and LOD of some reported carbon dots as probes for Fe3+ detection. Compared with these results, our assay had a relatively large advantage in sensitivity and reaction temperature to other methods and is capable of effectively detecting Fe3+ in real environmental water samples.

Conventional quenching mechanisms, including static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) have been widely studied. SQ occurs when a complex forms between the quencher and ground state fluorescent molecules through weak interactions, effectively suppressing fluorescence. DQ refers to the process in which the excited state molecules of a fluorescent substatnce lose their energy and return to the ground state through energy or charge transfer during collisions with the quenchers. The FRET process is a non-radiative process in which the energy of a donor is transferred to an acceptor by dipole–dipole interactions, and the distance between the two molecules is within 7 ∼ 10 nm. The FRET occurs when the emission spectrum of the donor molecule overlaps the excitation spectrum of the acceptor molecules. In contrast, the IFE requires a certain degree of spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quenchers and the excitation spectra or emission sepectra of fluorophore. [41,44,56].

To investigate the interaction mechanism between CDs and Fe3+, the time-resolved decay curve was measured to compare the average fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+. As presented in Fig. 9 A, with the addition of Fe3+, the average lifetime of CP11 decreased dramatically from 4.18 to 0.90 ns with fitting dual exponential decay traces, indicating the occurrence of dynamic quenching (DQ) [51,57]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 9B, the Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) on the concentration of Fe3+ ions were used to analyze the quenching constant. A good linear correlation was observed between the F0/F and the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 8–20 μM (F0/F = 0.1788[Fe3+] + 0.0167), suggesting that the Stern-Volmer quenching constant Ksv was 0.1788 μM−1 and the value of Kq was obtained as 4.28 × 1013 M−1S−1. Therefore, judging by the relatively high magnitude of Kq value (far greater than 1.0 × 1010 M−1S−1), it may be implied that the formation of a stable complex between CDs and quencher, leading to static quenching [53,70–72]. Meanwhile, the linear slope Ksv decreased with the increase of temperature (Fig. S7), which is consistent with the static quenching mechanism (SQ) [73–75]. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CP11 before and after addition of Fe3+ were displayed in Fig. 9C. It was found that a new absorption peak at about 491 nm appeared after the addition of Fe3+ in the CDs solution, which means the formation of complexes during the quenching process, the same as the previous result [40]. Besides, we further tested the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the excitation/emission spectra of CP11. As shown in Fig. 9D, the excitation of CP11 overlapped partly with the absorption of Fe3+, which may lead to inner filter effect (IFE) [4,49,52]. Overall, the interaction mechanism between CDs and Fe3+ might be a mixed synergistic process of dynamic quenching, static quenching and inner filter effect (Fig. 9E).

In summary, a facile one-step route for fabricating yellowish-green emissive CDs with the use coumarin and PEI as precursors is successfully demonstrated. The as-prepared CDs show a relatively high QY and yield when equal mass ratio of reactants was used. Owing to their intriguing optical and physical properties, pure-water CDs solutions were employed as staining reagent for the development of LFPs on various substrates with high-quality images including level 2 and level 3 information by simple immersing and spraying method. In addition, fluorescence 3D images were perceived that aid in fingermark recognition about the pressing habit of fingerprint owner. Thus, our present research provides an effective, rapid, large-scale and user-friendly means for visualized fingerprint dentification to solve the problem of the development of water-immersed LFPs and avoid the inhalation hazard of traditional fluorescent powder. Moreover, the pH-dependent fluorescence of CDs, makes it suitable as anti-counterfeiting ink. The desired information and pattern can be easily encrypted and decrypted through UV irradiation and acid/alkali treatment. Meanwhile, the CDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions in water samples, with potential application in environmental monitoring. The linear range for sensing Fe3+ was 0–27 μM with low LOD of 0.613 μM. More importantly, we conducted a careful study on the quenching mechanism by means of fluorescent lifetime, quenching rate constant, UV–Vis and excitation/emission spectra experiments. The results confirmed that the plausible mechanism might be a mixed synergistic process of dynamic quenching, static quenching and inner filter effect. Herein, our work provides a multifunctional CDs material, with broad applications in forensic science, information security, anti-counterfeiting and environmental analysis.
